---
title: "Iris Dataset practice"
author: "Brittnany Graham"
format: docx
editor: visual
---

## 💮Iris Dataset

The iris data set is a classic dataset in R, and contains the measurements of sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width of three species of iris flowers

There will be a few questions to help guide you through this workshop

### General Advice

When looking at questions through the workshop, my best advice is to understand that R is a tool to make a big picture.
Therefore, the question being asked is generally the big picture.
You, as a future coder and scientist is to understand the puzzle pieces to make the big picture come to life

RStudio is case sensitive, so you have to ensure when you are typing out code, that they match to what you're trying to pull from the dataset.
This will become apparent soon!

## General packages we will use

The tidyverse package houses multiple packages that is used frequently in RStudio, such as ggplot2, lubridate, dplyr, and purrr.
This is to avoid loading multiple libraries each time

```{r}
# install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)

green_checkmark <- insert_emoji("check")

# putting the iris dataset in a new object name
```

::: callout-note
## ⚠️Differences between warnings and errors

Warning are normally harmless in RStudio.
They are simply warning you of stuff R is doing behind the scenes.
However, and errors means something is critically wrong and R terminated the line of code you tried to run.
Error messages can sometimes be vague, but we can work through a few of them together.
:::

### Commonly used functions in tidyverse package

Again, there are many functions and packages housed within the tidyverse package, but one of packages being dplyr, which helps the user (you) manage and provide initial summaries of datasets.
For more information, check out the following link [dplyr package](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/)

**However:** I'll provide a few functions that will help you along in the exercise below

-   `str()` - stands for "structure" and it looks at the structure of the dataset

    -   Tells you how many variables is in the dataset

-   `head()` - gives you the first 5 - 10 rows of the dataset to give you an idea of what's going on

-   `dim()` - gives you the dimensions of the dataset

-   `distinct()` - selects first occurrence of data

-   `rename()` - renames columns

-   `select()` - select columns

-   `filter()` - filters rows

-   `arrange()` - sort data

-   `mutate()` - adds new data/manipulations and keeps old data

-   `transmute()` - adds new data/manipulations but only keeps new data

-   `group_by()` - groups data

-   `summarize()` - summary functions

## Task One: Explore the data

Take a moment and explore the Iris data set.
Play around with different functions!
What are some returns you get?
What are some questions that first pop into your mind?
What kind of structure is the data set?

```{r}
str(flowers)
head(flowers)
dim(flowers)

```

## Question 1

How many species of iris flowers in this data set?
What are their names?

*Hint:* start with creating an object to put iris in (name it something other than iris, get creative)

*Challenge:* If you want to rename the column names more intuitive, how would you do that?

```{r}

flowers <- iris

## head is to view the top 5/6 rows of a dataset 
head( )
dim( )


```

Answer here:

## Question 2

What is the average petal width of each species?

*Hint*: You'll want to subset each species into their own object names

`filter()` - function let's you pull out specific rows and columns and place them into a seperate object name

**Answer code:**

```{r}

unique(flowers$Species)

# subsetting the different species
setosa <- flowers %>%
  filter( )

versicolor <- flowers %>%
  filter( )

virginica <- flowers %>%
  filter ( )

## Next going to look at some summary stats for each species

summarize( )
summarize( )
summarize( )
```

## Small Intro to ggplot package

*`ggplot2`* is a graphing package from the tidyverse.
It's incredibly powerful for creating presentation ready and publication-quality graphics.
There are numerous types of graphs you can create, and you can add onto your code easily to make the graphics more complex.

Below are syntax definitions ggplot needs in order to work:

-   `ggplot()` - calls for a graph and you have to specify the data set you want to graph
-   `aes()` - aesthetics of the graph. This is where you define data for the x - axis and y - axis
    -   You can manipulate and change specific objects within the plot that we are interested in, such as shapes, colors, text size, legends, ect.
-   `labs()` - this is where you can change the `labels` on the graph. Such as x - axis, y - axis, title of the graph, and even add a subtitle
-   `theme()` - where you can "clean" the graph and take away the background grid.
    -   `theme_classic()` - gives the graph a blank background

    -   `theme_bw()` - gives the graph a black and white theme

**Next you need to know or understand different graphs within the ggplot package:**

-   `geom_point()` - scatter plot

-   `geom_line()` - line graph

-   `geom_bar()` - bar graph

-   `geom_hist()` - histogram

-   `geom_boxplot()` - box and whisker plot

There are so many graphs housed within the ggplot and these are the most common.
For a more comprehensive list, visit this comprehensive [ggplot2 guide](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/)!

## Question 3

This question is in two parts, don't worry, I will help you through each step!
The beauty of R is that once you do it once, you can modify and update the code to reflect what you need for a different project!

::: {#ggplot2}
This is where ggplot2 gets introduced!
It's a package within the tidyverse package, but it's government name is "Grammar of Graphics"

It's a way to create beautiful, publish quality plots in RStudio.

Talk more here about the structure of ggplot()
:::

First, create a scatter plot that showcases the relationship between sepal length and petal length for the entire data set

-   *Don't forget* break this big picture question into small puzzle pieces

**First bit**

```{r}
global_plot <- ggplot(  , aes(x = , y = , shape = , color = )) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_classic() +


global_plot
```

Looks, okay, right?
You can see the points, make some general assumptions about this graph, but we can spruce it up a bit!

Let's rename the axis titles and give this plot a title for a cleaner look

```{r}
global_plot2 <- ggplot(flowers, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Sepal.Length, shape = Species, color = Species)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(x = " ",
       y = " ",
       title = " ") +
  theme_classic()

global_plot2
```

There are many ways you can spruce up this graph!
You can change the shapes of each species, you can even change the color, change the position of the legend, capitalize the species name within the legend, and change the scale of the x and y axis.

If you are interested in how to do that, this link [R Graphics Cookbook 2nd Edition](https://r-graphics.org/) is a wonderful tool and reference.
I use it frequently when coding and making publishable graphs.

# Challenge on your own

![](images/palmerpenguins-02.png){fig-align="center" width="361"}

You can do this during the workshop and ask questions or on your own during your spare time!
If you have any questions, my email is bgraha33\@vols.utk.edu and you're more than welcome to reach out and ask questions!
Furthermore, after this workshop, I will share the document with full answers to the entire document

## Palmer's Penguins

![Meet the penguins!](images/penguins-01.png){fig-align="center" width="447"}

**Overview**: The Penguins dataset, provided by the `palmerpenguins` R package, is a widely used alternative to the classic `iris` dataset (which we were using previously).
It's designed for data exploration and visualization (perfect for this workshop).
The dataset contains measurements of three penguins - Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo - collected from three islands in the Palmer Archipeligo, Antarctica.

If you want the full vignette or instructions, visit the ["Introduction to palmerpenguins" website](https://allisonhorst.github.io/palmerpenguins/articles/intro.html#highlights)!

### Question 1

Explore the data!
First you have to install the `palmerpenguins` package, using the `install.packages()` function.

How many rows?
How many columns?
What are the names of the variables for the dataset?

**Answer here:**

```{r}

#install the package

install.packages('palmerpenguins')

## don't forget to 'shelve' it

library(palmerpenguins)

## Explore the data using one of functions listed above. Don't forget put the dataset in a new object name




```

::: callout-tip
## Handling NA's

**NA's** are missing values within the dataset and are very common when collecting data.
There are specific ways we handle these

This dataset is more realistic than the `iris` dataset because it has missing values which is what happens when collecting data sometimes.
I will provide the full code on how to delete rows or arrange rows with NA's
:::

```{r}
summary(antarctica)

## As you can see there are multiple NA's in some rows and we need to filter those out, so we will do it with the function `drop_na` which removes the rows with missing values to enable easy and accurate graphing

antarctica <- antarctica %>%
  drop_na() # this function takes out any rows containing the value NA

# the code to the left of the arrow is telling R: "take the dataset I've given you, take out rows that have any value in a cell that says NA, and then put it back into the same dataset"


## Now check again

summary(antarctica)

## As you can see the NA's have been removed from the dataset
```

::: callout-caution
## Applying this to the full dataset ⚠️

When you are manipulating data and are applying functions to the whole dataset ***be careful***.
R is unforgiving and if you accidentally do something that affects the whole dataset and alters it in a way you don't want it to, you will have to start over.

✅**Best Practice:** place your changes in a new object name, so if you do mess up, you can revert to the "previous version" of your dataset and won't have to start from scratch.
:::

## Question 2

Now I want you to create 3 graphs

1.  "Global Graph" that looks at bill length and bill depth across the 3 species (it'll be a scatter plot).
    1.  a\. I want your graph to have 3 things: clean labels, an informative title, and a clean background
    2.  a\. if you feel so inclined, change the colors using the function `scale_color_manual()` function and use the colors "dargeorange", "purple", "cyan4" (**Note:** you have to put names of colors in quotation marks. Also, feel free to utilize google, or the other resources provided such as the cookbook! If you're still struggling, raise your hand)
2.  A scatter plot looking at the body mass and flipper length across the three species
3.  Create a histogram of flipper length per species! I'll fill out the histogram portion myself and tell you what each line means

### Graph 1

Remember, you'll be using ggplot!

```{r}

ggplot(antarctica, aes(x = , y = , shape = , color = )) + # remember, you have three different species you want to decipher between
  geom_point(aes(color = species,
             shape = species),
             size = 2,
             alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("", "", "")) +
  labs(
    x = " ",
    y = " ",
    title = " "
  ) +
  theme_classic()
```

### Graph 2

```{r}


ggplot(antarctica, aes(x = , y = , shape = , color = )) + # remember, you have three different species you want to decipher between
  geom_point(aes(color = species,
                 shape = species),
             size = 2,
             alpha = 0.8) + 
  scale_color_manual(values = c(" ", " ", " ")) + # This is the same as above, but scale_color_manual can help you set the colors manually to whatever you want. 
  labs(x = " ",
       y = " ",
       title = " ") +
  theme_classic()
```

### Graph 3

This will be the histogram

```{r}

flipper_hist <- ggplot(antarctica, aes(x = )) + # you don't need a y - axis variable in a histogram because they are always either counts or frequency within the data
  geom_histogram(aes(fill = species),
                 alpha = 0.5, 
                 position = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("darkorange", "purple", "cyan4")) +
  labs(x = " ",
       y = " ", # On the y - axis on histograms they are either frequencies or counts of data
       title = " ") +
  theme_bw()

flipper_hist
```
